hints: Each term is x₁^{e₁}…x₆^{e₆} with e₁+…+e₆=18.
Count nonnegative solutions: stars and bars.
solution: Distinct terms correspond to exponent tuples with $e_1+\dots+e_6=18$, of which there are $\binom{18+5}{5}=\binom{23}{5}=33649.$
Over $3$ square yards imperfections are $\mathrm{Poisson}(12)$, so $P(\ge1)=1-e^{-12}=1+\dfrac{-1}{e^{12}}$. Thus $a=-1,b=12$ and $a+b=11.$

hints: By radial symmetry the angle is uniform; P(X+Y>0)=1/2.
X>0 and X+Y>0 is the angular wedge from −45° to 90° (135°).
solution: By radial symmetry the direction is uniform. $X+Y>0$ is a half-plane (prob $\tfrac12$), and $X>0\cap X+Y>0$ is the wedge of angles $(-45^\circ,90^\circ)$, i.e. $135^\circ=\tfrac38$ of the circle. So the conditional probability is $\dfrac{3/8}{1/2}=\dfrac34.$

hints: List the 4 equally likely outcomes and their payoffs.
x³ ends in 11 ⟺ x³ ≡ 11 (mod 100); only x mod 100 matters.
Cubing is a bijection mod 25 (gcd(3,20)=1) and forces a unique residue mod 4, hence mod 100.
solution: We need $x^3\equiv11\pmod{100}$, which depends only on $x\bmod100$. Mod $25$, cubing is a bijection (since $\gcd(3,20)=1$), giving one solution; combined with the forced residue mod $4$, exactly one residue mod $100$ works. So the probability is $\tfrac1{100}=0.01.$

hints: By symmetry P(B)=P(C), and P(B)+P(C)+P(E)=1, so we need P(E)=1/3.

solution: The characteristic equation is $\lambda^2-(\mathrm{tr})\lambda+\det=\lambda^2+\lambda-6=0$, so $\lambda=2,-3$. Then $a^2+b^2=4+9=13.$

hints: Model strength as balls (or Exp lifetimes); winner inheriting strength means the overall winner is decided by total strength.
Bob wins with probability (Bob's total)/(grand total).
solution: Represent each unit of strength as a ball; the rule 'winner with prob $\tfrac{a}{a+b}$ and inherits strength' makes the tournament equivalent to drawing balls so that the side owning the very last ball wins. That side is Bob with probability $\dfrac{\text{Bob's total}}{\text{grand total}}=\dfrac{30}{40}=\dfrac34.$

hints: Track heads−tails; it's a symmetric walk with absorbing barriers +10 and −7.
Expected time to hit a barrier from 0 is a·b.
solution: The difference heads$-$tails is a symmetric walk absorbed at $+10$ and $-7$. Starting at $0$, the expected time to hit a barrier is $a\cdot b=10\cdot7=70.$

hints: Put one good candy alone in a box; the rest (4 good, 5 bad) in the other.
solution: Place a single good candy in one box and the other $4$ good $+5$ bad in the second. Then $P(\text{good})=\tfrac12\cdot1+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac49=\tfrac9{18}+\tfrac4{18}=\tfrac{13}{18}.$

hints: All odd moments of a standard normal are 0.
